v_k_chaithanya_varma_chintalapati.name: V K Chaithanya Varma Chintalapati
v_k_chaithanya_varma_chintalapati.summary: Hailing from Oregon, V K Chaithanya Varma Chintalapati is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is V's.
v_k_chaithanya_varma_chintalapati.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for V K Chaithanya Varma Chintalapati, from Oregon, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and V is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 NW OLC, and so far, the only thing V has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The V saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

V is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be V's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or V is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

V is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says V wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, V, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

vaasvik_potluri.summary:
A Georgia competitor, Vaasvik Potluri is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U11 level, with 3 entries across 1 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Vaasvik competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
vaasvik_potluri.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vaasvik Potluri, from Georgia, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Vaasvik is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Fortius South CRC, and so far, the only thing Vaasvik has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Vaasvik saga began at 2025 Fortius South CRC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vaasvik plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vaasvik has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vaasvik showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 Fortius South CRC in BS U11, which means Vaasvik traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Vaasvik is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Vaasvik wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Vaasvik, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

vaibhav_reddy_kommareddy.name: Vaibhav Reddy Kommareddy
vaibhav_reddy_kommareddy.summary:
A Texas competitor, Vaibhav Reddy Kommareddy is a committed competitor who keeps showing up, currently competing at the U15 level, with 20 career entries across 9 tournaments. In a sport that rewards persistence, Vaibhav's commitment is the ultimate investment in future success.

A true triple-threat, Vaibhav competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Vaibhav has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Aditee Kulkarni (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Bhavesh SAI VARMA Tammu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events)

Vaibhav has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Vaibhav has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Vaibhav brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
vaibhav_reddy_kommareddy.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vaibhav Reddy Kommareddy, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vaibhav has 20 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vaibhav saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vaibhav plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Vaibhav has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Aditee Kulkarni (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Bhavesh SAI VARMA Tammu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Vaibhav has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vaibhav is not just losing locally — Vaibhav is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kommareddy family travels cross-country so Vaibhav can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vaibhav has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vaibhav showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15, which means Vaibhav traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Vaibhav entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 49th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Vaibhav paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Vaibhav keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Vaibhav has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Vaibhav, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Vaibhav. We will be here, taking notes.

vaishnavi_packiyaraj.name: Vaishnavi Packiyaraj

A true triple-threat, Vaishnavi competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 3 singles titles, a doubles best of #2, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Vaishnavi has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:

Vaishnavi has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Vaishnavi has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Vaishnavi consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 5 titles, Vaishnavi has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vaishnavi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vaishnavi has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Vaishnavi does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Vaishnavi has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 5 wins means Vaishnavi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vaishnavi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 0 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Vaishnavi can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Vaishnavi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vaishnavi's texts. The go-to partner is Bryce Xie (10 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11 — we hope Bryce Xie has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Vaishnavi's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Vaishnavi has competed 6 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one. Tournament committees have handed Vaishnavi a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Vaishnavi has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2024 NE NJBC CRC in GS U13, where Vaishnavi was seeded #2 and finished 4th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vaishnavi is not just losing locally — Vaishnavi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Packiyaraj family travels cross-country so Vaishnavi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vaishnavi has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vaishnavi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11, which means Vaishnavi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Vaishnavi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Vaishnavi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 44 entries, and 39 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vaishnavi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vaishnavi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vaishnavi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vaishnavi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

valarie_jiao.name: Valarie Jiao
valarie_jiao.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Valarie Jiao is a tenacious competitor with 3 quarterfinal-or-better finishes, currently competing at the U19 level, building a strong foundation across 5 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

A true triple-threat, Valarie competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Valarie brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
valarie_jiao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Valarie Jiao, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Valarie's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Valarie keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Valarie saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Valarie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Valarie has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U19 — we hope Maanas Mallela has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Valarie has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Valarie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U19, which means Valarie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

Season after season, Valarie keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Valarie has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Valarie, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Valarie. We will be here, taking notes.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Varad isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Varad's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
varad_v_vaishnav.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Varad V Vaishnav, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Varad's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Varad keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Varad saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Varad plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Varad has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Dhev Sudheer has emotionally recovered.

Varad has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Varad has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Varad has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Varad showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Varad traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Varad entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Varad paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Varad keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Varad has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Varad, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Varad. We will be here, taking notes.

varin_karri.name: Varin Karri
varin_karri.summary:
Out of Texas, Varin Karri knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 72 entries across 31 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

What makes Varin especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (7 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries).

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Varin adapts to anyone:
  • Tatthva Sharma (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (6 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Dwijesh Makireddi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Renee Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Varin has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Varin has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Varin is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
varin_karri.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Varin Karri, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 72 career entries in 31 tournaments, Varin has 1 title — a 1% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 71 entries where Varin drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 72 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Varin saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2025 Austin Leander South CRC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Varin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 18 entries — a stretch where Varin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Varin is 0-for-10. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Varin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Varin is 0-for-26. Zero titles in 26 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Varin and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Varin needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Varin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Varin's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aashi Gupta: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aashi Gupta has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U15 — we hope Advik Kevlani has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Varin's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Varin entered 7 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Varin's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Varin and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Varin has shown up 8 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Varin is not just losing locally — Varin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 1% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Karri family travels cross-country so Varin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Varin has 45 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Varin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Varin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Varin entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Varin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 72 entries, and 71 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Varin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Varin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Varin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Varin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

varish_karri.name: Varish Karri
varish_karri.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Varish Karri has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U13 BS, U13 XD, U13 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 23 career titles across 32 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Varish is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Varish especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 6 singles titles, 9 doubles titles, 7 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U13 BS, U13 XD, U13 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U13, BD U11, BD U13) • 9 ORC titles.

Varish has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Keira Winarto (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (18 total events in U13/XD, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Shaurya Maddela (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (5 events, best: #3)
  • Kavya Balaji (current partner) — 2 events in XD, producing 2 titles together, across 2 seasons
  • Taneesh Musunuru — 12 events in BD/U13, producing 5 titles together, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Varish is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Varish has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Varish has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Varish has been seeded #1 or #2 in 21 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 23 titles, Varish has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
varish_karri.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Varish Karri, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Varish is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Varish has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Varish would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Varish, we kept reading — all 91 entries across 32 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 25% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Varish held up a trophy, there were roughly 2 other times Varish walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Varish saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 South Frisco ORC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Varish's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 7 titles in 16 entries — a stretch where Varish actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 15 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Varish experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Varish has reached 12 championship matches across this career. That is 12 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 12 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (9 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Varish does own 23 actual titles, so it is not like Varish has never won anything. But 12 finals losses versus 23 wins means Varish is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Varish plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 6 singles, 9 doubles, 7 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Varish can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Varish has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Varish's texts. The go-to partner is Keira Winarto (18 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U13 — we hope Keira Winarto has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Varish has been seeded in 32 events and underperformed the seeding 12 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Varish takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Varish. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Varish of is playing it safe. Varish has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Varish has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 10 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Varish has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Varish is not just losing locally — Varish is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 25% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Karri family travels cross-country so Varish can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 U.S. Selection Event. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Varish has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Varish showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U13 BS, which means Varish traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Selection Event, where Varish entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Varish paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 23 titles to the name, 91 entries, and 68 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Varish looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Varish's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Varish has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Varish. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

varshini_subramanian.name: Varshini Subramanian
varshini_subramanian.summary:
A California competitor, Varshini Subramanian has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 37 entries in 14 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Varshini is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

A veteran of the junior circuit, Varshini has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Varshini has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Varshini brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
varshini_subramanian.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Varshini Subramanian, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Varshini has 37 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Varshini saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Varshini plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 37 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Varshini has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Varshini's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Shriariman Karthigayan: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Shriariman Karthigayan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Lokesh Ravuri has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Varshini has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Varshini is not just losing locally — Varshini is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Subramanian family travels cross-country so Varshini can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Varshini has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Varshini showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U15, which means Varshini traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Varshini entered 2 events with results ranging from 65th to 65th. That is an average placement of 65th, which is a polite way of saying Varshini paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 37 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Varshini. Varshini just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Varshini has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Varshini. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Varshini: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

vaughn_zhao.name: Vaughn Zhao
vaughn_zhao.summary:
Out of California, Vaughn Zhao has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U19 XD, U15 BS, U15 XD, U15 BD, U19 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 6 career titles across 19 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Vaughn is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Vaughn is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U19 XD, U15 BS, U15 XD, U15 BD, U19 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 4 ORC titles.

With 19 different doubles partners over the years, Vaughn adapts to anyone:
  • Suraj Rajesh Kumar (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (3 total events in BD)
  • Jennifer Xu (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Kevin Zhou — a trusted BD/U19 partner (4 events, best: #3)
  • Jasmine La — 3 events in U19/XD, best: #4

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 3 titles across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Vaughn is still ascending.

Vaughn has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vaughn has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Vaughn consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 6 titles, Vaughn has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
vaughn_zhao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vaughn Zhao, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Vaughn is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Vaughn has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Vaughn would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Vaughn, we kept reading — all 52 entries across 19 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 12% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Vaughn held up a trophy, there were roughly 7 other times Vaughn walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Vaughn saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vaughn's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 8 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vaughn experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vaughn has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Vaughn does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Vaughn has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 6 wins means Vaughn is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vaughn plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Vaughn can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Vaughn has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vaughn's texts. The go-to partner is Caden Wang (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15 — we hope Jaleesa Yu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Vaughn has shown up 3 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

One thing nobody can accuse Vaughn of is playing it safe. Vaughn has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Vaughn has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 3 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Vaughn has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vaughn is not just losing locally — Vaughn is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhao family travels cross-country so Vaughn can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vaughn has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vaughn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15, which means Vaughn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event in U19 BS. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Vaughn entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Vaughn paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 52 entries, and 46 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vaughn looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vaughn's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vaughn has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vaughn. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ved_vedere.name: Ved Vedere
ved_vedere.summary:
Hailing from California, Ved Vedere has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-4 in BD U19. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Ved belongs with the best in the country.

Notably, Ved has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Ved is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (7 entries) • Junior Nationals top-4 (BD U19) • 1 ORC title.

Ved has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Thanik Bharani (current primary partner) — 13 events, 1 title together in BD/U19, spanning 3 seasons
  • Wenlu She — a trusted U19/XD partner (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Ved has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Ved has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Ved is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
ved_vedere.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ved Vedere, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 32 career entries in 14 tournaments, Ved has 1 title — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 31 entries where Ved drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 32 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Ved saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ved's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 15 entries — a stretch where Ved actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Ved is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ved plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Ved is 0-for-10. Zero titles in 10 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Ved and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Ved needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. The go-to partner is Thanik Bharani (13 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U19 — we hope Wenlu She has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ved entered 7 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Ved's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Ved and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Ved 4 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Ved has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ved is not just losing locally — Ved is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Vedere family travels cross-country so Ved can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ved has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ved showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U17, which means Ved traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U19 BS. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Selection Event, where Ved entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Ved paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 32 entries, and 31 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ved looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ved's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ved has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ved. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Hailing from Texas, Veda Gayatri Mullangi is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U19 level, with 12 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Veda especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (1 entry) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Veda has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Veda brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
veda_gayatri_mullangi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Veda Gayatri Mullangi, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 12 career entries in 6 tournaments, Veda has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 11 entries where Veda drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 12 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Veda saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Veda plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Veda has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Abel Daniel Koshy has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Veda has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Veda has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Veda has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Veda showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Veda traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 South Frisco ORC, where Veda entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Veda paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Veda keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Veda has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Veda, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Veda. We will be here, taking notes.

veda_r_edula.name: Veda R Edula
veda_r_edula.summary:
Out of Texas, Veda R Edula is just getting started on the junior circuit with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Veda's.

Veda competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
veda_r_edula.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Veda R Edula, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Veda is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 DFW South OLC, and so far, the only thing Veda has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Veda saga began at 2024 DFW South OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Veda has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Veda showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2024 DFW South OLC in GS U13, which means Veda traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Veda is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Veda wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Veda, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

vedaant_sreedharan.name: Vedaant Sreedharan
vedaant_sreedharan.summary:
Representing Missouri, Vedaant Sreedharan broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 23 entries across 11 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

What makes Vedaant especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Vedaant adapts to anyone:
  • Felix Zhang (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (7 total events in BD, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Connor Mills (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Vedaant has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vedaant has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Vedaant is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
vedaant_sreedharan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vedaant Sreedharan, from Missouri, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vedaant has 23 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vedaant saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vedaant's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 9 entries — a stretch where Vedaant actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vedaant is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vedaant plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Vedaant is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Vedaant and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Vedaant needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Vedaant has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Felix Zhang (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U17 — we hope Felix Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Vedaant's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Vedaant has shown up 2 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vedaant is not just losing locally — Vedaant is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sreedharan family travels cross-country so Vedaant can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vedaant has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vedaant showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Vedaant traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Vedaant entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Vedaant paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 23 entries, and 22 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vedaant looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vedaant's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vedaant has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vedaant. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

  • Jonathan Alexander surya Putra (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #4)
