vedasri_saanvi_puppala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vedasri Saanvi Puppala, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 22 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 22-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Vedasri's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Vedasri keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Vedasri saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Vedasri would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Vedasri has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Vedasri has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Vedasri's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vedasri plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 22 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Vedasri has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vedasri's texts. The go-to partner is William Lin (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Jonathan Alexander surya Putra has emotionally recovered.

Vedasri has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Vedasri has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vedasri has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vedasri showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U11, which means Vedasri traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Vedasri entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Vedasri paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Vedasri keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Vedasri has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Vedasri, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Vedasri. We will be here, taking notes.

vedhanth_kumar_kanthala.name: Vedhanth Kumar Kanthala
vedhanth_kumar_kanthala.summary:
Out of Texas, Vedhanth Kumar Kanthala is relentlessly knocking on the door, currently competing at the U13 level, reaching 3 finals across 31 entries. A player who reaches that many championship matches has all the tools — the breakthrough title is coming.

What makes Vedhanth especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Vedhanth adapts to anyone:
  • Manvi Jandhyala (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2)
  • Gautam Vasanth Raj (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #5, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Kyle Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #3)

Vedhanth has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Vedhanth has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Vedhanth brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
vedhanth_kumar_kanthala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vedhanth Kumar Kanthala, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-33. Top-33 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 31 career entries in 14 tournaments, Vedhanth has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 30 entries where Vedhanth drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 31 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Vedhanth saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vedhanth experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vedhanth has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. And remember: Vedhanth has ZERO titles. None. 3 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vedhanth plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 31 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Vedhanth has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vedhanth's texts. The go-to partner is Gautam Vasanth Raj (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Gautam Vasanth Raj has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Vedhanth entered 2 events with results ranging from top-33 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Vedhanth's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Vedhanth and said 'nah.'

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vedhanth is not just losing locally — Vedhanth is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kanthala family travels cross-country so Vedhanth can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 U.S. Selection Event. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vedhanth has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vedhanth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U13 BS, which means Vedhanth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Vedhanth entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Vedhanth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 31 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Vedhanth. Vedhanth just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Vedhanth has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Vedhanth. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Vedhanth: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

venba_senkadir.name: Venba Senkadir
venba_senkadir.summary:
Hailing from California, Venba Senkadir has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Venba's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

What makes Venba especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Venba adapts to anyone:
  • Shreya Rajesh Kumar (current primary partner) — 9 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (9 total events in GD, across 2 seasons)
  • Devaansh Mahato (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (7 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Iniyan Arun (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Having moved up to U13, Venba is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Venba brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Venba has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Venba has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Venba brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
venba_senkadir.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Venba Senkadir, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-6. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Venba has 39 career entries across 13 tournaments and has managed to win 5 of them — a 13% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Venba saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Venba's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 5 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Venba actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Venba is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Venba experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Venba has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC, where Venba entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U11, GD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Venba does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Venba has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 5 wins means Venba is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Venba plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Venba can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Venba has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Venba's texts. The go-to partner is Shreya Rajesh Kumar (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11 — we hope David Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Venba's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Venba has shown up 3 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Venba is not just losing locally — Venba is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Senkadir family travels cross-country so Venba can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Venba has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Venba showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U11, which means Venba traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Venba entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Venba paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 39 entries, and 34 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Venba looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Venba's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Venba has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Venba. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

venbha_sathish_kumar.name: Venbha Sathish Kumar
venbha_sathish_kumar.summary:
Representing Georgia, Venbha Sathish Kumar has stepped onto the competitive stage with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Venbha's.

Venbha competes in both singles and mixed doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
venbha_sathish_kumar.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Venbha Sathish Kumar, from Georgia, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Venbha is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Fortius South CRC, and so far, the only thing Venbha has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Venbha saga began at 2025 Fortius South CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Venbha is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Venbha wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Venbha, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

venkata_sahasra_kondaveeti.name: Venkata Sahasra Kondaveeti
venkata_sahasra_kondaveeti.summary:
Out of Virginia, Venkata Sahasra Kondaveeti embodies the relentless spirit of junior badminton, currently competing at the U15 level, with 30 career entries across 21 tournaments. In a sport that rewards persistence, Venkata's commitment is the ultimate investment in future success.

Notably, Venkata has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Venkata especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (1 entry).

Venkata has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Venkata has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Venkata brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

venkata_taneesh_chavvakula.name: Venkata Taneesh Chavvakula
venkata_taneesh_chavvakula.summary: Representing Texas, Venkata Taneesh Chavvakula is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Venkata's.
venkata_taneesh_chavvakula.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Venkata Taneesh Chavvakula, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Venkata is right on schedule. It all started at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, and so far, the only thing Venkata has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Venkata saga began at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Venkata is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Venkata's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Venkata is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Venkata has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Venkata showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U11, which means Venkata traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Venkata is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Venkata wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Venkata, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

venya_pamidimukkala.name: Venya Pamidimukkala
venya_pamidimukkala.summary:
Representing Illinois, Venya Pamidimukkala is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U13 level, reaching the final four 3 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

A true triple-threat, Venya competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Venya has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Venya brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
venya_pamidimukkala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Venya Pamidimukkala, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 9 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Venya is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC, and so far, the only thing Venya has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Venya saga began at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Venya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 9 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Venya has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.'

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Venya has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Venya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in GS U11, which means Venya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Season after season, Venya keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Venya has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Venya, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Venya. We will be here, taking notes.

vera_lin.name: Vera Lin
vera_lin.summary:
Representing California, Vera Lin has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in GD U15, XD U15. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Vera belongs with the best in the country.

Notably, Vera has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Vera is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (GD U15, XD U15) • 2 ORC titles.

Vera has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Kevin Zhou (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Hebe Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Weslie Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (2 events)
  • Carryssa Caryn Bun (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in GD/U17

The 2021-2022 season tells a story of acceleration, with 3 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Vera is still ascending.

Vera has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NW, NorCal, SoCal, Vera isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Vera's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
vera_lin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vera Lin, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 17 career entries in 6 tournaments, Vera has 3 titles — a 18% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 4 entries where Vera drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 17 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Vera saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2021 NorCal CRC in GS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vera's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2021-2022 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 14 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vera plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Vera can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Vera entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Vera's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Vera and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Vera 3 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Vera has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Vera has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vera has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vera showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U17, which means Vera traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U17 XD. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 17 entries, and 14 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vera looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vera's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vera has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vera. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

veronica_carol_ornatsky.name: Veronica Carol Ornatsky
veronica_carol_ornatsky.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Veronica Carol Ornatsky has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 4 entries in 2 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Veronica competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).
veronica_carol_ornatsky.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Veronica Carol Ornatsky, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Veronica has 4 career entries across 2 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Veronica saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

At Junior Nationals, Veronica has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Veronica has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Veronica showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15, which means Veronica traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Veronica entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Veronica paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Veronica keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Veronica has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Veronica, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Veronica. We will be here, taking notes.

veronica_li.summary:
Veronica Li is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U17 level, with 3 entries across 1 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

What makes Veronica especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

veronica_ornatsky.name: Veronica Ornatsky
veronica_ornatsky.summary:
A Illinois competitor, Veronica Ornatsky is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 2 titles across 16 tournaments and 42 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Veronica keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Veronica has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Veronica especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Veronica adapts to anyone:
  • Connor Mills — a trusted XD partner (6 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Veronica has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Veronica isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 5 seasons and 2 titles, Veronica has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
veronica_ornatsky.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Veronica Ornatsky, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 2 wins across 42 entries in 16 tournaments. That is a 5% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Veronica shows up to a tournament, Veronica pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Veronica saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2022 Midwest CRC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Veronica's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 9 entries — a stretch where Veronica actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Veronica is 0-for-1. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Veronica experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Veronica has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Veronica does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Veronica has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Veronica is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Veronica plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Veronica is 0-for-16. Zero titles in 16 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Veronica and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Veronica needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Veronica has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Veronica's texts. The go-to partner is Nivedya S Nair (8 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Aaron Jin has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Veronica's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Veronica has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Veronica has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Veronica has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Veronica showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Veronica traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Veronica entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Veronica paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 42 entries, and 40 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Veronica looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Veronica's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Veronica has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Veronica. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Winning at the Selection Event (U19 GS, U19 XD, U19 GD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 8 career titles across 8 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

What makes Veronica especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U19 GS, U19 XD, U19 GD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (GS U19, GD U19) • 4 ORC titles.

victor_dubuc.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Victor Dubuc, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-49. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Victor has 5 career entries across 3 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Victor saga began at 2024 NE NJBC CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

At Junior Nationals, Victor has shown up 1 time with a best of top-49. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Victor has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Victor showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Victor traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 NE MassBad OLC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 NE MassBad OLC, where Victor entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Victor paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Victor is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Victor wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Victor, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

victoria_a_liu.name: Victoria A Liu
victoria_a_liu.summary:
Hailing from Minnesota, Victoria A Liu has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Victoria's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Victoria is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Victoria especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Victoria adapts to anyone:
  • Lukas Ye (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (10 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Kaydence Cheung (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #4)

Victoria has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, SoCal, Victoria isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Victoria brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
victoria_a_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Victoria A Liu, from Minnesota, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Victoria has 35 career entries across 13 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Victoria saga began at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Victoria plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 35 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Victoria has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Victoria's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Lukas Ye: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Lukas Ye has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Lukas Ye has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Victoria has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Victoria has also played up into older age groups at 5 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Victoria has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Victoria has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Victoria showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Victoria traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Victoria entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Victoria paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Victoria keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Victoria has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Victoria, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Victoria. We will be here, taking notes.

vidhu_reddy_keesara.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vidhu Reddy Keesara, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vidhu has 30 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vidhu saga began at 2024 DFW South OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vidhu plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 30 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Vidhu has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vidhu's texts. The go-to partner is Yize Han (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Lohitaksh Chandu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Vidhu has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Vidhu paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Vidhu keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Vidhu has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. We will be here, taking notes.

vignan_chandrasekaran.name: Vignan Chandrasekaran
vignan_chandrasekaran.summary:
Out of Texas, Vignan Chandrasekaran has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, capturing an impressive 6 ORC titles across 4 regional championships. ORCs are the highest level of regular-season competition, drawing the strongest fields in each region. Winning at this level repeatedly marks Vignan as one of the premier players on the circuit.

Vignan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 4 singles titles, 9 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • 6 ORC titles.

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Vignan adapts to anyone:
  • Michael Yufu Sun (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (6 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Sophia Z Liu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5)
  • Aayush Jaladurgam (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (3 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 10 podium finishes across 6 tournaments. Vignan is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Vignan has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Vignan has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 14 seeded entries across the career, Vignan consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 15 titles, Vignan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
vignan_chandrasekaran.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vignan Chandrasekaran, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 65 career entries in 22 tournaments, Vignan has 15 titles — a 23% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 3 entries where Vignan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 65 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Vignan saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2024 DFW South OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vignan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vignan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vignan has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Vignan does own 15 actual titles, so it is not like Vignan has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 15 wins means Vignan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vignan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 9 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Vignan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Vignan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vignan's texts. The go-to partner is Reyansh Chalamalasetti (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U11 — we hope Reyansh Chalamalasetti has emotionally recovered.

Of Vignan's 15 career titles, 9 came at local events versus 6 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Vignan feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Vignan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Vignan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Vignan and said 'nah.'

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vignan is not just losing locally — Vignan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 23% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chandrasekaran family travels cross-country so Vignan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vignan has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vignan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U11, which means Vignan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event, where Vignan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Vignan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 15 titles to the name, 65 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vignan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vignan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vignan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vignan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

  • Manas Chintala — 6 events, 1 title together in BD, spanning 3 seasons

Vignesh has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vignesh has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 2 titles, Vignesh has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
vignesh_jami.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vignesh Jami, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vignesh has 69 career entries across 25 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vignesh saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vignesh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 18 entries — a stretch where Vignesh actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vignesh is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vignesh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Vignesh is 0-for-25. Zero titles in 25 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Vignesh and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Vignesh needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 24 of them, to be precise. Vignesh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vignesh's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Andy W Gao: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Andy W Gao has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Vidushi Karn has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Vignesh has shown up 9 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vignesh is not just losing locally — Vignesh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jami family travels cross-country so Vignesh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vignesh has 45 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vignesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15, which means Vignesh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Vignesh entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Vignesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 69 entries, and 67 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vignesh looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vignesh's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vignesh has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vignesh. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet.
