We will be here, taking notes.

vihaan_darbha.name: Vihaan Darbha
vihaan_darbha.summary:
Out of North Carolina, Vihaan Darbha has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Vihaan's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Vihaan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Vihaan adapts to anyone:
  • Ananya Mishra (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Emilyn Lizbeth Joseph (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Tanay Kankanala — a trusted BD partner (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Vihaan has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vihaan has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Vihaan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
vihaan_darbha.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vihaan Darbha, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vihaan has 64 career entries across 23 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vihaan saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Houston CRC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vihaan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 16 entries — a stretch where Vihaan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vihaan is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vihaan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vihaan has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 South CRC (TBTT), where Vihaan entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Vihaan does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Vihaan has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 1 wins means Vihaan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vihaan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Vihaan is 0-for-23. Zero titles in 23 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Vihaan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Vihaan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Vihaan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vihaan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Adhip Sundar: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Adhip Sundar has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Adhip Sundar has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Vihaan's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Vihaan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vihaan is not just losing locally — Vihaan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Darbha family travels cross-country so Vihaan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vihaan has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vihaan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Vihaan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Vihaan entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Vihaan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 64 entries, and 63 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vihaan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vihaan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vihaan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vihaan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

vihaan_garre.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vihaan GARRE, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Vihaan is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 ABC OLC, and so far, the only thing Vihaan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Vihaan saga began at 2024 ABC OLC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Vihaan is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Vihaan's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Vihaan is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vihaan has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vihaan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 ABC OLC in BS U15, which means Vihaan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Vihaan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Vihaan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Vihaan, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

Hailing from California, Vihaan Vinod has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 36 entries across 16 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Vihaan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Ishaan A Pillutla (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (9 events, across 2 seasons)

Vihaan has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vihaan has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Vihaan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
vihaan_vinod.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vihaan Vinod, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vihaan has 36 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vihaan saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 1 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in BD U15 - Position 5-8, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vihaan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 19 entries — a stretch where Vihaan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vihaan is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vihaan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Vihaan is 0-for-16. Zero titles in 16 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Vihaan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Vihaan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Vihaan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vihaan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ishaan A Pillutla: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ishaan A Pillutla has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Sanjanaa V Priya has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Vihaan's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Vihaan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vihaan is not just losing locally — Vihaan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Vinod family travels cross-country so Vihaan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vihaan has 29 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vihaan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U15, which means Vihaan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Vihaan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Vihaan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 36 entries, and 35 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vihaan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vihaan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vihaan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vihaan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Vimala has 12 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vimala saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

At Junior Nationals, Vimala has shown up 2 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

vincent_chen.name: Vincent Chen
vincent_chen.summary:
Out of Illinois, Vincent Chen has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U13 BS, U13 BD, U17 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 19 career titles across 38 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Vincent is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Vincent is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 6 singles titles, 11 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U13 BS, U13 BD, U17 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (BD U15) • 8 ORC titles.

With 24 different doubles partners over the years, Vincent adapts to anyone:
  • Jennifer Bangcaya (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (6 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Saatvik Shetty (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (5 total events in BD/U17, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 8 podium finishes across 4 tournaments. Vincent is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Vincent has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vincent has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Vincent has been seeded #1 or #2 in 13 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 6 seasons and 19 titles, Vincent has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
vincent_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vincent Chen, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Vincent is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Vincent has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Vincent would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Vincent, we kept reading — all 100 entries across 38 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 19% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Vincent held up a trophy, there were roughly 4 other times Vincent walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Vincent saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2021 Egret Midwest OLC in BS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vincent's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 9 titles in 26 entries — a stretch where Vincent actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 3 wins in 10 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vincent experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vincent has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Vincent entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U17, BD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Vincent does own 19 actual titles, so it is not like Vincent has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 19 wins means Vincent is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vincent plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 6 singles, 11 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Vincent can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 24 of them, to be precise. Vincent has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vincent's texts. The most successful partnership is with Aarya Vijay — 15 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Aarya Vijay deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Aarya Vijay a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Daby Huang has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Vincent has been seeded in 31 events and underperformed the seeding 9 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Vincent takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15, and finishing 65th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Vincent. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Vincent of is playing it safe. Vincent has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Vincent has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 11 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Vincent has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vincent is not just losing locally — Vincent is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 19% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Vincent can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vincent has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vincent showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Vincent traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Vincent entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 45th, which is a polite way of saying Vincent paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 19 titles to the name, 100 entries, and 81 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vincent looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vincent's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vincent has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vincent. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The Vincent saga began at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vincent plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 23 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Vincent has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vincent's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ethan Yichen Zhou: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ethan Yichen Zhou has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Elsa Anqing Wang has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Vincent has shown up 2 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vincent is not just losing locally — Vincent is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Vincent can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vincent has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vincent showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Vincent traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Vincent entered 2 events with results ranging from 13th to 49th. That is an average placement of 31st, which is a polite way of saying Vincent paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Vincent keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Vincent has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Vincent, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Vincent. We will be here, taking notes.

vindhya_chekuru.name: Vindhya Chekuru
vindhya_chekuru.summary:
Out of Virginia, Vindhya Chekuru has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Vindhya is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Vindhya is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries) • 1 ORC title.

With 24 different doubles partners over the years, Vindhya adapts to anyone:
  • Trisha Mehta (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Arnav Satish (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Vindhya has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vindhya has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Vindhya consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 3 titles, Vindhya has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
vindhya_chekuru.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vindhya Chekuru, from Virginia, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 69 career entries in 25 tournaments, Vindhya has 3 titles — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 22 entries where Vindhya drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 69 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Vindhya saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 Midwest OLC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vindhya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Vindhya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vindhya is 0-for-10. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vindhya experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vindhya has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Vindhya does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Vindhya has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 3 wins means Vindhya is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vindhya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Vindhya can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 24 of them, to be precise. Vindhya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vindhya's texts. The go-to partner is Anirudh Rudharraju (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Adhip Sundar has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Vindhya entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Vindhya's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Vindhya and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Vindhya has shown up 5 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Vindhya of is playing it safe. Vindhya has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Vindhya has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Vindhya has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vindhya is not just losing locally — Vindhya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chekuru family travels cross-country so Vindhya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vindhya has 28 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vindhya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U15, which means Vindhya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Vindhya entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Vindhya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 69 entries, and 66 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vindhya looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vindhya's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vindhya has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vindhya. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Virat plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 4 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Virat can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Virat has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Virat's texts. The most successful partnership is with Laasya Polineni — 12 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Laasya Polineni deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Laasya Polineni a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11 — we hope Kayal Selvamanickam has emotionally recovered.

Of Virat's 7 career titles, 4 came at local events versus 3 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Virat feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Virat entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Virat's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Virat and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Virat has been seeded in 13 events and underperformed the seeding 6 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Virat takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #4 at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in BS U11, and finishing 33rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Virat. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Virat is not just losing locally — Virat is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Satish family travels cross-country so Virat can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Virat has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Virat showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Virat traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Virat entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Virat paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 56 entries, and 49 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Virat looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Virat's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Virat has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Virat. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Either nobody wants to be Viren's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Viren is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Viren is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Viren wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Viren, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

vishaant_harish.name: Vishaant Harish
vishaant_harish.summary:
A Texas competitor, Vishaant Harish has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Vishaant's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Vishaant is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: ORC top-2 finisher.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Vishaant adapts to anyone:
  • Kayden Hossain (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #2)
  • Sophia Li (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Vishaant is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 2 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Vishaant is still ascending.

Vishaant has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Vishaant has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Vishaant's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
vishaant_harish.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vishaant Harish, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 wins across 21 entries in 9 tournaments. That is a 10% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Vishaant shows up to a tournament, Vishaant pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Vishaant saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 1 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26) in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vishaant's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 16 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vishaant experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vishaant has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Austin Leander South OLC, where Vishaant entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U15, XD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Vishaant does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Vishaant has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Vishaant is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vishaant plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Vishaant can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Vishaant has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vishaant's texts. The go-to partner is Kayden Hossain (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U15 — we hope Jonah Chenfei Wong has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Vishaant's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vishaant is not just losing locally — Vishaant is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Harish family travels cross-country so Vishaant can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vishaant has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vishaant showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Vishaant traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Vishaant entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Vishaant paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 21 entries, and 19 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vishaant looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vishaant's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vishaant has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vishaant. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

vishanth_vijay.name: Vishanth Vijay
vishanth_vijay.summary:
Hailing from Georgia, Vishanth Vijay is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 2 titles across 24 tournaments and 56 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Vishanth keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Vishanth is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Vishanth especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (7 entries).

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Vishanth adapts to anyone:
  • Lokesh Ravuri (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2)
  • Mithun Gopinath — a trusted BD partner (7 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Siya Alicia Chen — 5 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Vishanth has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vishanth has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Vishanth is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
vishanth_vijay.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vishanth Vijay, from Georgia, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vishanth has 56 career entries across 24 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vishanth saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vishanth's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 16 entries — a stretch where Vishanth actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vishanth is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Vishanth would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Vishanth has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Vishanth has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Vishanth's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vishanth plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Vishanth can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Vishanth has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vishanth's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Mithun Gopinath: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Mithun Gopinath has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U15 — we hope Siddharth Sriganesh has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Vishanth's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Vishanth has shown up 7 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Vishanth of is playing it safe. Vishanth has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17 — which means Vishanth has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Vishanth has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vishanth is not just losing locally — Vishanth is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Vijay family travels cross-country so Vishanth can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vishanth has 37 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vishanth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Vishanth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Vishanth entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Vishanth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 56 entries, and 54 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vishanth looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vishanth's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vishanth has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vishanth. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

vishnu_a.name: Vishnu A
vishnu_a.summary:
Out of California, Vishnu A has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, capturing an impressive 5 ORC titles across 4 regional championships. ORCs are the highest level of regular-season competition, drawing the strongest fields in each region. Winning at this level repeatedly marks Vishnu as one of the premier players on the circuit.

Notably, Vishnu has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Vishnu is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 4 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (8 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 5 ORC titles.

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Vishnu adapts to anyone:
  • nandYeewei Yap (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • nandSitar Sanghani (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • nandPrathik Nemani (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 2 titles across 7 events in BD/U13, spanning 4 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Vishnu has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vishnu has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 13 seeded entries across the career, Vishnu consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 5 titles, Vishnu has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
vishnu_a.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vishnu A, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 64 career entries in 24 tournaments, Vishnu has 5 titles — a 8% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 11 entries where Vishnu drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 64 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Vishnu saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 NW ORC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vishnu's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Vishnu actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vishnu is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vishnu experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vishnu has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Vishnu does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Vishnu has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 5 wins means Vishnu is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vishnu plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Vishnu is 0-for-21. Zero titles in 21 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Vishnu and suddenly the wins start appearing — 5 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Vishnu needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Vishnu has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vishnu's texts. The go-to partner is nandPrathik Nemani (7 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope nandKavya Goyal has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Vishnu entered 8 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Vishnu's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Vishnu and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Vishnu has shown up 9 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

Vishnu has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vishnu is not just losing locally — Vishnu is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The A family travels cross-country so Vishnu can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vishnu has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vishnu showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Vishnu traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Vishnu entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Vishnu paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 64 entries, and 59 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vishnu looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vishnu's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vishnu has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vishnu. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Hailing from North Carolina, Vishnu Basava has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 18 entries in 9 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Vishnu competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Vishnu adapts to anyone:
  • Ishan Kanchi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (5 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

Vishnu has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Vishnu brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
vishnu_basava.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vishnu Basava, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 18 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 18-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Vishnu's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Vishnu keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Vishnu saga began at 2024 South CRC (TBTT) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

On the doubles front, Vishnu has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Ishan Kanchi: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Vishnu paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Vishnu keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Vishnu has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. We will be here, taking notes.
