vishnu_pilla.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vishnu Pilla, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vishnu has 5 career entries across 2 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vishnu saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

At Junior Nationals, Vishnu has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Vishnu has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vishnu has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vishnu showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Vishnu traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Vishnu entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 49th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Vishnu paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Vishnu keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Vishnu has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Vishnu, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Vishnu. We will be here, taking notes.

vishnu_reddy_jallu.name: Vishnu Reddy JALLU
vishnu_reddy_jallu.summary: Vishnu Reddy JALLU is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Vishnu's.
vishnu_reddy_jallu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vishnu Reddy JALLU, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Vishnu is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 ABC OLC, and so far, the only thing Vishnu has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Vishnu saga began at 2024 ABC OLC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Vishnu is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Vishnu's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Vishnu is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vishnu has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vishnu showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 ABC OLC in BS U17, which means Vishnu traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Vishnu is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Vishnu wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Vishnu, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

vishvarupa_sivam.name: Vishvarupa Sivam
vishvarupa_sivam.summary:
A Maryland competitor, Vishvarupa Sivam is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U19 level, with 12 entries across 7 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Vishvarupa is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Vishvarupa is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Vishvarupa has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Vishvarupa isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Vishvarupa brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

vishwa_rajan.name: Vishwa Rajan
vishwa_rajan.summary:
Hailing from Georgia, Vishwa Rajan has tasted victory multiple times, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 2 titles across 19 tournaments and 46 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Vishwa keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Vishwa has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Vishwa competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Vishwa adapts to anyone:
  • Mukil Nambikumar (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (5 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)

Vishwa has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Vishwa has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Vishwa is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
vishwa_rajan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vishwa Rajan, from Georgia, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vishwa has 46 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vishwa saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vishwa's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 11 entries — a stretch where Vishwa actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vishwa is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Vishwa would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Vishwa has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Vishwa has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Vishwa's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vishwa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Vishwa is 0-for-19. Zero titles in 19 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Vishwa and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Vishwa needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Vishwa has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vishwa's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Mithun Gopinath: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Mithun Gopinath has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Radhika Birur has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Vishwa's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Vishwa has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Vishwa has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vishwa is not just losing locally — Vishwa is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Rajan family travels cross-country so Vishwa can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vishwa has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vishwa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Vishwa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Vishwa entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Vishwa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 46 entries, and 44 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vishwa looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vishwa's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vishwa has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vishwa. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Vishwajeeth has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Vishwajeeth isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Vishwajeeth has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? So from the bottom of our hearts, Vishwajeeth: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

vivaan_chada.name: Vivaan Chada
vivaan_chada.summary:
Representing Texas, Vivaan Chada keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 4 times, currently competing at the U15 level, building a strong foundation across 10 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

What makes Vivaan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Vivaan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Arjun Urankar (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Soudamini Reddy (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #5)

Vivaan has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Vivaan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
vivaan_chada.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vivaan Chada, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vivaan has 26 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vivaan saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vivaan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 26 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Vivaan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vivaan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Arjun Urankar: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Arjun Urankar has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Reya Santhosh has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Vivaan has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Vivaan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Vivaan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Vivaan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Vivaan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Vivaan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Vivaan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Vivaan. We will be here, taking notes.

vivaan_khullar.name: Vivaan Khullar
vivaan_khullar.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Vivaan Khullar has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 37 entries across 15 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Vivaan has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Vivaan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 12 different doubles partners over the years, Vivaan adapts to anyone:
  • Brighton Yu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (6 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Kennis He (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Agastya Kalal (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)

Vivaan has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Vivaan isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Vivaan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
vivaan_khullar.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vivaan Khullar, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Vivaan has 37 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Vivaan saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 Midwest CRC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vivaan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 14 entries — a stretch where Vivaan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vivaan is 0-for-14. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vivaan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Vivaan is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Vivaan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Vivaan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Vivaan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vivaan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Brighton Yu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Brighton Yu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Chelsie Cheng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Vivaan's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Vivaan has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Vivaan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Vivaan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vivaan has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vivaan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Vivaan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Vivaan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Vivaan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 37 entries, and 36 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vivaan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vivaan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vivaan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vivaan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15, which means Vivek traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Vivek entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Vivek paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Vivek keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Vivek has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Vivek, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Vivek. We will be here, taking notes.

vivek_r_annapureddy.name: Vivek R Annapureddy
vivek_r_annapureddy.summary:
Vivek R Annapureddy is just getting started on the junior circuit with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Vivek's.

Vivek competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
vivek_r_annapureddy.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vivek R Annapureddy, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Vivek is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, and so far, the only thing Vivek has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Vivek saga began at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vivek has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vivek showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Vivek traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Vivek entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Vivek paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Vivek is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Vivek wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Vivek, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

vivek_rai.name: Vivek Rai
vivek_rai.summary:
Representing California, Vivek Rai is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Vivek in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Vivek is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Vivek is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U15 BD, U17 BS, U17 BD) • Junior Nationals champion (XD U17) • 3 ORC titles.

With 18 different doubles partners over the years, Vivek adapts to anyone:
  • Sasha Suman (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Arush Shyam Chitgopkar (current partner) — 3 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons
  • Nihal Kadamba (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Akshay Jalwadi — 3 events in BD/U17, best: #2

A veteran of the junior circuit, Vivek has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vivek has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 19 seeded entries across the career, Vivek consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 6 seasons and 4 titles, Vivek has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
vivek_rai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vivek Rai, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. And yes, Vivek IS a Junior National Champion — XD U17 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 69-entry career across 27 tournaments with a 6% win rate, which means 65 times — 65! — Vivek went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 69 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Vivek saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vivek's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Vivek actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vivek is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vivek experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vivek has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 SoCal ORC, where Vivek entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U15, BD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Vivek does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Vivek has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 4 wins means Vivek is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vivek plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Vivek can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Vivek has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vivek's texts. The go-to partner is Max Zhang (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Samanvi Boppana has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Vivek entered 8 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Vivek's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Vivek and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Vivek has been seeded in 19 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Vivek takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #5 at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in BS U17, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Vivek. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Vivek of is playing it safe. Vivek has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Vivek has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 3 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Vivek has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vivek is not just losing locally — Vivek is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Rai family travels cross-country so Vivek can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vivek has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vivek showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17, which means Vivek traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Vivek entered 3 events with results ranging from 3rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Vivek paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 69 entries, and 65 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vivek looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vivek's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vivek has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vivek. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

vivian_feng.name: Vivian Feng
vivian_feng.summary:
Representing New Jersey, Vivian Feng has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 GD, U15 XD, U15 GS, U13 GD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 19 career titles across 33 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Vivian is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (7 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Vivian especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 9 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 4 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 GD, U15 XD, U15 GS, U13 GD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals top-2 (GD U17, GS U11, GD U13, XD U15, GD U11 - Position 1-4) • 15 ORC titles.

With 25 different doubles partners over the years, Vivian adapts to anyone:

  • Michelle Yourong Zhang (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (8 total events in GD/U15, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 8 podium finishes across 4 tournaments. Vivian is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Vivian has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vivian has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Vivian has been seeded #1 or #2 in 7 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 19 titles, Vivian is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.

vivian_liu.name: Vivian Liu
vivian_liu.summary:
Out of Illinois, Vivian Liu has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 20 entries in 8 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

A true triple-threat, Vivian competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

And yet, Vivian keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Vivian saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vivian plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Vivian has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vivian's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Aditya Agarwal has emotionally recovered.

Vivian has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Vivian has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vivian has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vivian showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Vivian traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Vivian entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Vivian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Vivian keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Vivian has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Vivian, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Vivian. We will be here, taking notes.

vivian_shi.name: Vivian Shi
vivian_shi.summary:
A California competitor, Vivian Shi is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Vivian in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Vivian has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Vivian competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U17 GD, U17 GS) • Junior Nationals champion (GD U17) • 1 ORC title.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Vivian adapts to anyone:
  • Kevin Zhou (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/U19/XD (9 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Jasmine La (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U19 (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Angela Ye — 5 events, 1 title together in GD/U17, across 2 seasons

Vivian has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Vivian has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Vivian is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
vivian_shi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Vivian Shi, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. And yes, Vivian IS a Junior National Champion — GD U17 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 57-entry career across 19 tournaments with a 4% win rate, which means 55 times — 55! — Vivian went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 57 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Vivian saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 NW ORC in GD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Vivian's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Vivian actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Vivian is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Vivian experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Vivian has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Vivian does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Vivian has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 2 wins means Vivian is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Vivian plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Vivian is 0-for-16. Zero titles in 16 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Vivian and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Vivian needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Vivian has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Vivian's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Kevin Zhou: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Kevin Zhou has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Daniel Wu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Vivian entered 9 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Vivian's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Vivian and said 'nah.'

Vivian has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Vivian is not just losing locally — Vivian is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shi family travels cross-country so Vivian can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Vivian has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Vivian showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15, which means Vivian traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Vivian entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Vivian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 57 entries, and 55 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Vivian looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Vivian's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Vivian has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Vivian. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Vyom entered 2 events with results ranging from 49th to 49th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Vyom paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Vyom keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Vyom has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Vyom, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Vyom. We will be here, taking notes.
